title: Comparative Analysis of Adaptation Criteria for Gradient-Based Discrete MCMC: When Acceptance-Rate Trumps Jump-Distance
description: We propose the first systematic comparison of adaptive gradient-based discrete MCMC methods, specifically evaluating acceptance-rate-based adaptation (ALBP, Sun et al. 2022) against jump-distance maximization (AB-sampler, Sun et al. 2023a) and cyclical scheduling (ACS, Pynadath et al. 2024). Rather than proposing a new algorithm, we provide empirical characterization of failure modes—particularly near phase transitions—and practical guidance for method selection based on problem characteristics. We also provide CPU-focused wall-clock benchmarks using the same problem instances as DISCS for direct comparability.
motivation: Recent years have seen multiple adaptive discrete MCMC methods: ALBP (Sun et al. 2022) targeting acceptance rate 0.574, AB-sampler (Sun et al. 2023a) maximizing jump distance, and ACS (Pynadath et al. 2024) using cyclical schedules. However, no systematic comparison exists evaluating which adaptation criterion works best under what conditions. Each paper evaluates on different problems with different metrics, leaving practitioners without guidance. Moreover, failure modes are poorly understood: Sun et al. (2023a) note their adaptation is 'not stable until the chain reaches stationarity,' and Roberts & Rosenthal (2001) note optimal scaling theory fails at phase transitions. We fill this gap by providing the first head-to-head comparison with explicit failure mode analysis and statistical rigor (effect sizes, power analysis).
proposed_approach: We pursue five thrusts: (1) Pilot validation: Reproduce Sun et al. (2022) results on tractable problems to validate our implementation before large-scale experiments. (2) Head-to-head comparison: Evaluate ALBP (acceptance-rate), AB-sampler (jump-distance), and ACS (cyclical) under unified framework with identical evaluation metrics and problem sets, plus additional baselines (fixed-step GWG and grid-search heuristic). (3) Statistical rigor: Effect size analysis (Cohen's d), power analysis for Mann-Whitney U tests, and meaningful difference thresholds (>0.5 SD). (4) Failure mode analysis: Explicit characterization of when each method fails, including phase transition stress tests, pre-convergence adaptation instability, and multimodality challenges. (5) CPU benchmark: Practical wall-clock comparisons using the same problem instances as DISCS for direct comparability. We do NOT propose a new algorithm—we contribute understanding through systematic comparison.
hypothesis: Acceptance-rate-based adaptation (ALBP) provides superior robustness compared to jump-distance maximization (AB-sampler) for gradient-based discrete MCMC, particularly in high-dimensional correlated distributions, but both methods fail near phase transitions where local correlation structure invalidates theoretical assumptions. Cyclical scheduling (ACS) provides advantages for multimodal distributions where fixed-target adaptation may trap in local modes.

confirmed: ALBP achieves ≥80% of optimal ESS on ≥70% of problems with <20% variance across seeds (rationale: Sun et al. 2022 notes efficiency remains high for acceptance rates in [0.5, 0.7])
AB-sampler shows >2× higher variance in early adaptation phase compared to ALBP (validates Sun et al. 2023a's instability claim)
Both methods show >50% performance degradation at critical temperature compared to off-critical (validates Roberts & Rosenthal 2001 warning)
ACS discovers ≥2× more modes than ALBP/AB-sampler on multimodal targets
Clear decision criteria emerge for method selection based on problem characteristics
refuted: Different methods perform best on different problems with no systematic pattern
AB-sampler consistently outperforms ALBP across problem types
Empirically optimal acceptance rates differ systematically from 0.574 even on product distributions
success_criteria.mixed_results_handling: Mixed results (e.g., ALBP wins on correlated problems, AB-sampler on product distributions, ACS on multimodal targets) are still valuable scientific contributions providing nuanced guidance for practitioners and identifying boundary conditions for theoretical predictions.

statistical_rigor.effect_size: Cohen's d > 0.5 (medium effect) for meaningful differences
statistical_rigor.power_analysis: n=10 seeds provides 80% power to detect medium effects at α=0.05
statistical_rigor.significance: p < 0.05 with Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons

novel_findings: Quantitative characterization of how phase transitions affect adaptive methods
Minimum adaptation steps needed for stability guidelines
Clear problem characteristics predicting which adaptation criterion works best
Grid-search heuristic comparison: whether simple heuristics match complex adaptive methods

related_work.paper: Sun et al. (2022) - Optimal scaling for locally balanced proposals in discrete spaces
related_work.authors: Haoran Sun, Hanjun Dai, Dale Schuurmans
related_work.key_contribution: Established optimal acceptance rate ρ* = 0.574 for locally-balanced proposals. Proposed ALBP with stochastic approximation adaptation (Eq. 22). Validated on Bernoulli, Ising, FHMM, RBM.
related_work.how_ours_differs: We explicitly acknowledge their priority on acceptance-rate-based adaptation. We do NOT claim novelty in this regard. Instead, we compare their ALBP against other adaptive approaches (AB-sampler, ACS) and characterize failure modes they identified but did not fully analyze (phase transitions). We add statistical rigor (effect sizes, power analysis).

related_work.paper: Sun et al. (2023a) - Any-scale balanced samplers for discrete space
related_work.authors: Haoran Sun, Bo Dai, Charles Sutton, Dale Schuurmans, Hanjun Dai
related_work.key_contribution: AB-sampler adaptively tunes step size σ and balancing parameter α via jump-distance maximization. Uses quadratic approximation and Gaussian integral trick.
related_work.how_ours_differs: We implement their AB-sampler for comparison. We explicitly test their acknowledged limitation: 'adaptation is not stable until the chain reaches stationarity.' We compare head-to-head against ALBP to determine when jump-distance vs. acceptance-rate adaptation works better. We include additional baselines for contextualization.

related_work.paper: Pynadath et al. (2024) - Gradient-based Discrete Sampling with Automatic Cyclical Scheduling
related_work.authors: Patrick Pynadath, Riddhiman Bhattacharya, Arun Hariharan, Ruqi Zhang
related_work.key_contribution: ACS uses cyclical step size and balancing schedules with acceptance-rate-based automatic tuning. Targets multimodal distributions with non-asymptotic convergence guarantees.
related_work.how_ours_differs: We include ACS in our comparison to characterize when cyclical scheduling is necessary vs. monotonic adaptation. We evaluate whether the added complexity provides benefits for non-multimodal problems.

related_work.paper: Grathwohl et al. (2021) - Oops I took a gradient: Scalable sampling for discrete distributions
related_work.authors: Will Grathwohl, Kevin Swersky, Milad Hashemi, David Duvenaud, Chris J. Maddison
related_work.key_contribution: Introduced Gibbs-With-Gradients (GWG), the first gradient-based discrete sampler using locally-balanced proposals. Foundation for subsequent adaptive methods.
related_work.how_ours_differs: We use fixed-step GWG as a baseline to contextualize the value of adaptation. Our grid-search heuristic baseline also builds on GWG proposals.

related_work.paper: Rhodes & Gutmann (2022) - Enhanced gradient-based MCMC in discrete spaces
related_work.key_contribution: Introduced NCG and PAVG samplers. Appendix E.1 includes AdaptGamma algorithm for adaptive step-size tuning via jump distance maximization.
related_work.how_ours_differs: We acknowledge their adaptive method. Our focus is on comparing the two dominant modern approaches (ALBP's acceptance-rate targeting vs. AB-sampler's jump-distance maximization) with additional statistical rigor.

related_work.paper: Roberts & Rosenthal (2001) - Optimal scaling for various Metropolis-Hastings algorithms
related_work.authors: Gareth O. Roberts, Jeffrey S. Rosenthal
related_work.key_contribution: Established optimal acceptance rate theory for continuous spaces. Explicitly notes theory 'fails when the target distribution has phase-transition behavior.'
related_work.how_ours_differs: We empirically validate this warning in the discrete setting. While Roberts & Rosenthal noted the theoretical limitation, no systematic study has characterized how adaptive discrete samplers fail near phase transitions.

related_work.paper: Goshvadi et al. (2023) - DISCS: A Benchmark for Discrete Sampling
related_work.authors: Katayoon Goshvadi, Haoran Sun, Xingchao Liu, Azade Nova, Ruqi Zhang, Will Grathwohl, Dale Schuurmans, Hanjun Dai
related_work.key_contribution: First comprehensive benchmark for discrete sampling. Implements GWG, PAS, DLMC, etc. on GPU. Uses standardized problem instances (Lattice Ising, Random Ising, RBM).
related_work.how_ours_differs: We use the same problem instances as DISCS for direct comparability. We extend DISCS by: (1) evaluating adaptive variants not in the original benchmark, (2) CPU-focused wall-clock measurements, (3) failure mode analysis, (4) systematic comparison of adaptation criteria with statistical rigor.

phase1_pilot.description: Validate theoretical predictions on small tractable problems before large-scale experiments
experiments: Reproduce Sun et al. (2022) Figure 5: Verify optimal acceptance rate 0.574 on Bernoulli model
Pilot comparison: ALBP vs. AB-sampler on 100D Gaussian with ground truth
success_criteria: Reproduce key results from Sun et al. (2022) within ±0.05 acceptance rate
Both adaptive methods converge to near-optimal parameters
Pilot confirms feasibility of larger experiments

phase2_comparison.description: Head-to-head comparison of adaptation criteria under identical conditions with statistical rigor
problems: Lattice Ising (100-900 variables): Below, at, and above critical temperature
Random Ising (100-500 variables): Frustrated interactions, glassy landscapes

statistical_analysis.effect_size: Cohen's d for ESS differences between methods
statistical_analysis.power_analysis: Post-hoc power analysis for Mann-Whitney U tests
statistical_analysis.meaningful_difference: >0.5 standard deviations in ESS (medium effect size)

key_questions: Does ALBP maintain near-0.574 acceptance rate across problem types?
Does AB-sampler achieve higher jump distance but lower ESS?
Which method is more robust to initialization?
Does the simple grid-search heuristic match adaptive methods?

experiments: Phase Transition Stress Test: Ising at critical temperature (J = 0.44 for 2D lattice)
Pre-Convergence Adaptation: Start from 'bad' initializations, measure stability
Multimodality: Mixture of Gaussians in discrete space (2-10 modes)
expected_findings: Both methods struggle at critical temperature; acceptance rate deviates from 0.574
AB-sampler more unstable early; ALBP more robust
ACS discovers more modes; fixed-target methods may trap

phase4_cpu_benchmark.description: Practical guidance for CPU-only practitioners using same instances as DISCS
phase4_cpu_benchmark.discs_comparability: Uses same problem instances as Goshvadi et al. (2023) for direct comparability. Where DISCS reports GPU wall-clock times, we report CPU wall-clock times.
measurements: Total time = adaptation + sampling to target ESS
deliverables: Decision tree: 'If your problem is X, use method Y'
CPU vs GPU comparison showing where CPU is viable

fixed_step_gwg.description: Standard Gibbs-With-Gradients with fixed step size σ = 0.1
fixed_step_gwg.rationale: Provides baseline showing value of adaptation

grid_search_heuristic.description: Run short pilot chains with step sizes σ ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5}, select best by empirical jump distance, then freeze
grid_search_heuristic.rationale: Tests whether complex online adaptation is necessary or if simple heuristics suffice

statistical_rigor.power: 80% power to detect medium effects (d=0.5) at α=0.05
statistical_rigor.justification: Based on statistical best practices for comparing algorithms (Demšar 2006, JMLR)

computational_requirements.feasibility: All experiments are analytical/algorithmic, tractable on CPU. No neural network training required. Pilot validation in Phase 1 mitigates risk. Grid-search heuristic is simpler than full adaptive methods.

contingency_plan.minimal_viable: Phase 1 + Phase 2 on subset of problems

expected_outputs: Comparative analysis paper with systematic evaluation of adaptive criteria
CPU-optimized implementations of ALBP, AB-sampler, and ACS under unified framework
Grid-search heuristic baseline implementation for comparison
Failure mode characterization for adaptive discrete MCMC
Practitioner guidelines for method selection with statistical rigor
impact: First systematic comparison of adaptive discrete MCMC criteria with statistical rigor
Empirical characterization of failure modes, particularly near phase transitions
Validation (or refutation) of theoretical predictions in practical settings
CPU-focused benchmark using same instances as DISCS for direct comparability
Framework for handling mixed results in algorithm comparison studies

positioning_vs_prior_adaptive_work.summary: We explicitly acknowledge prior adaptive discrete MCMC work. We do NOT claim algorithmic novelty.
positioning_vs_prior_adaptive_work.what_sun_2022_did: Proved optimal acceptance rate ρ* = 0.574; proposed ALBP with stochastic approximation adaptation (Eq. 22)
positioning_vs_prior_adaptive_work.what_sun_2023a_did: Proposed AB-sampler with jump-distance maximization; noted adaptation instability before stationarity
positioning_vs_prior_adaptive_work.what_pynadath_2024_did: Proposed ACS with cyclical schedules for multimodality; acceptance-rate-based tuning
what_we_do: Systematic comparison: First head-to-head evaluation of all three approaches
Additional baselines: Grid-search heuristic to contextualize adaptive methods
Statistical rigor: Effect sizes and power analysis
Failure mode analysis: Explicit characterization of when each method fails
Mixed results handling: Framework for interpreting non-binary outcomes
DISCS comparability: CPU benchmark using same problem instances
explicit_non_claims: We do NOT claim to be the first to propose acceptance-rate-based adaptation (Sun et al. 2022 did this)
We do NOT claim a new adaptive algorithm (we compare existing ones)
We do NOT claim new theoretical optimal rates (we empirically validate existing theory)
We DO NOT claim novelty in failure mode analysis exists—we claim it is understudied and we contribute systematic characterization

feedback_addressed.statistical_rigor: Added effect size analysis (Cohen's d), power analysis, meaningful difference thresholds, and Bonferroni correction
feedback_addressed.additional_baselines: Added fixed-step GWG and grid-search heuristic baseline beyond fixed-step methods
feedback_addressed.mixed_results_handling: Explicitly discussed that mixed results (e.g., ALBP wins on some problem classes, AB-sampler on others) are valuable scientific contributions providing nuanced guidance
feedback_addressed.discs_comparability: Clarified that CPU benchmark uses same problem instances as DISCS for direct comparability
feedback_addressed.timeline: Added contingency plan with prioritized experiments (essential vs. optional) and minimal viable experiment set
feedback_addressed.novelty: Strengthened positioning as first systematic comparison with statistical rigor, acknowledging empirical comparison is the contribution
feedback_addressed.interaction_effects: Added discussion of how adaptation criteria interact with balancing function type, local vs. global proposals, and warmup strategies
feedback_addressed.success_criteria_justification: Added statistical justification for thresholds (80% ESS based on Sun et al. 2022 robustness; 70% coverage allowing for known failure modes; Cohen's d conventions)
